data.title: Sol-Gel Technologies: Downgrading To 'Hold' As Success Relies On Upcoming Q4 Catalyst
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Sol-Gel Technologies (SLGL), it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled “Sol-Gel Shines In Treating 2 Skin Disorders, NDAs Expected For Both Indications In 2020.” With respect to this article, I noted that the company was gearing up to receive FDA approvals for its drugs TWYNEO for acne vulgaris in adults and pediatric patients ages 9 and older and EPSOLAY for the treatment of inflammatory lesions for adults with Rosacea. Both of these drugs are alright but are no longer the key drivers of value for the company that investors should be looking at. A second update is that this company has been hard at work developing its topical hedgehog signaling pathway inhibitor SGT-610 for patients with Gorlin Syndrome.

The point of this drug is to block the mutation of the PTCH1 gene, which allows new tumors to grow. This particular candidate, helping this group of skin disorder patients, is already being evaluated in a phase 3 study. I bring this up because there is a major inflection point at the end of this year (Q4 of 2026), which would be the expected release of topline results from this late-stage study of SGT-610. It is an inflection point in the sense that, besides possibly generating shareholder value (if positive data is announced), it would also serve to allow the company to expand to another indication by the name of high-frequency basal cell carcinoma (BCC).

This is crucial because even though it is a rare disease, it is said that even if only going after severe forms of these BCC patients, it could double the market potential of SGT-610. Again, this other indication is not to be pursued until the results from the phase 3 study using this drug to treat patients with Gorlin Syndrome are known. With not much traction from ESPOLAY and TWYNEO over the years, plus the reliance on a major data readout from a phase 3 study in Q4 of 2026, I believe that I should downgrade Sol-Gel Technologies' stock to a “Hold” rating.

Q4 2026 Is Major Inflection Point For Sol-Gel As A Biotech

First and foremost, I believe that it is important to go over what Gorlin Syndrome is. But before that, it's important to highlight that should the company be able to somehow pull off a win in the phase 3 study using SGT-610 to treat patients with Gorlin Syndrome, then it believes that it can more than double the market potential for SGT-610 (even if it only goes after severe forms of BCC formations).

Gorlin Syndrome ((GS)), also known as nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome (NBCC), is a rare genetic disorder causing the formation of various benign and cancerous tumors. This autosomal dominant genetic disorder is caused by the inheritance of the tumor suppressor patched homolog 1 (PTCH1) gene. Why is a genetic mutation of this PTCH1 gene a bad thing? That's because it is important in stopping or preventing the smoothened receptor gene (SMO) from occurring. With the stoppage of this receptor gene, the Hedgehog signaling pathway is shut down when not needed and doesn't lead to BCC cell tumor cells dividing and growing uncontrollably. Thus, SGT-610 is a Hedgehog signaling pathway inhibitor that is responsible for not allowing the SMO receptor to lead to the formation of new BCCs in patients.

In order to see whether or not SGT-610 (Patidegib Gel 2%) is going to be effective in preventing the formation of BCCs on a patient's face with GS, the company is currently in the process of evaluating it in a phase 3 study. This late-stage trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 140 patients and then randomize them 1:1 to receive either of the following doses:

  • SGT-610 twice daily (BID dosing)
  • Vehicle Gel BID dosing

With everything I have described above, it comes down to the established primary efficacy endpoint of this study, which is to assess BCCs. That is, at the end of the 12-month period, there will be an assessment of the number of new BCCs formed between those who took SGT-610 and those who took Vehicle Gel. The downside to this program is that a clinical data readout is still very far away. This is because topline results from this phase 3 study, using this Hedgehog signaling pathway inhibitor to treat patients with GS, are not expected until Q4 of 2026. The company is first awaiting to see results from this study before deciding if it wants to move towards the expansion indication of HF-BCC. Should the phase 3 GS study meet the primary endpoint and establish positive data, then the company believes that it is going to be able to move forward with the expansion. With that being said, it is possible that it could initiate a phase 3 study using SGT-610 to treat patients with HF-BCC in 2027.

Financials

According to the 6-K SEC filing, Sol-Gel Technologies had cash, cash equivalents, and deposits of $6.8 million and marketable securities of $14.1 million as of September 30, 2025. This brings a total cash balance of $20.9 million as of this date. This is a problem in that even though the company has two approved products, it is not generating a lot of cash from them. The stock price has increased recently, recovering from past losses, but it was likely on the basis of the advancement of the phase 3 study of SGT-610 for the treatment of patients with GS. In terms of cash, the company is low, and I expect it to raise cash either before the release of phase 3 GS data or afterwards.

Either way, it has a cash crunch and needs to find new funds quickly. It is not just on something that I am projecting myself. This is something that the company notes itself, which is that it believes it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into Q1 of 2027. As far as TWYNEO and ESPOLAY go, the company had made agreements for these products with various companies across the globe. It is expected to launch them in many new territories in 2027 and 2028. Still, it does not expect to have a $10 million annual royalty stream of revenue for these products until 2031 and beyond. The company burns about $6.7 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $5.7 million in R&D expenses and then $1 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sol-Gel Technologies. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the ongoing evaluation of SGT-610 for the treatment of patients with Gorlin Syndrome. The thing is that the primary endpoint for this study is the number of new BCCs on the face at Month 12, comparing SGT-610 to vehicle gel. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company's drug is going to do better than the vehicle at preventing the formation of new BCCs at month 12. Nor that the data to be released by the end of 2026 is going to allow it to be in a position to file for regulatory approval for it.

The second risk to consider would be that of the potential expansion opportunity of SGT-610, which is to possibly be used to treat patients with high-frequency BCC. The notion here is that there was high demand, and that since this drug could potentially work in treating Gorlin Syndrome, then it might also be effective in treating patients with high-frequency BCC. The risk here is that even though it has been given to a non-Gorlin patient in France with a severe form of high-frequency BCC, it doesn't automatically mean that it will work out well for many other patients. In addition, this expansion opportunity of targeting BCC is only to happen if the phase 3 study targeting Gorlin Syndrome patients with SGT-610 is positive. If a negative data readout happens, not only would it cause the company's stock to trade lower, but it would also mean no expansion into high-frequency BCC.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the low amount of cash on hand that it has. The reason why I'm stating this is because it has $20.9 million in cash as of September 30, 2025. Again, it believes that it is only going to be able to fund its operations into Q1 of 2027. This means it is going to have to find a way to raise cash in the next several months. This could include many things, like selling shares, taking on debt, issuing warrants, or other financial tools at its disposal. The only way that it could possibly avoid a cash raise is if it develops a partnership agreement with another company. However, this is not guaranteed to happen.

Conclusion

Sol-Gel Technologies has gained back some of its stock price that it lost since 2020. It is a promising biotech, but I think it's riskier than most others because there is not a sufficient number of backup pipeline products. If you look at its pipeline, it is quite bare, especially since it had failed to achieve positive results in a phase 1b study using SGT-210 for Darier disease. It might look at targeting other indications, but I believe it would only do so via investigator-initiated trials because it doesn't have a lot of cash on hand. Thus, everything for this company hinges on the phase 3 data in Q4 of 2026. If it hits, great, but if it misses, I'm going to give my honest opinion that it could end up filing for bankruptcy as the likely scenario.

data.title: Silver: My 2017 Thesis On Industrial Demand, Illustrates Why This Is Not A Silver Market Bubble
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It is easy to correlate the meteoric rise in silver prices with the dramatic rally in gold prices we saw in the past few years. There is a strong argument to be made, given that gold and silver prices have been moving in the same direction historically. Silver market dynamics are, in fact, very different from those of gold, because a rising share of silver demand comes from industrial applications, many of which do not allow for effective recovery of the silver content used in producing various products. The rising demand for industrial applications is coming close to matching total global mined output.

I wrote an article in early 2017 where I meant to illustrate just how much potential industrial demand there is on the planet, by pointing out that if the world were to hypothetically have to run exclusively on solar power, there would not be enough silver, already mined and above ground, as well as known reserves that are yet to be mined to make a world powered exclusively by solar power possible.

Because silver prices are up about fivefold since I wrote that article, and I expect to see further robust gains in the price of this metal, I want to revisit my reasoning behind continuing a buy & hold position, rather than take profits as I ordinarily tend to do when an investment accrues such gains. The main silver position in my stock portfolio is the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV), which is a convenient way to gain exposure to the silver market, on short notice, in reaction to increasingly volatile market moves. I am currently adding to my existing position on the dips, using my low initial entry point as leverage to manage downside risks.

About the SLV ETF

The iShares Silver Trust ETF is designed to match the performance of the silver spot price. It has an expense ratio of .5% and $41 billion in assets, in the form of 16,348 tonnes of silver.

As we can see from the chart, the ETF will slightly underperform the spot silver price in the longer term, due to factors such as the expense involved. There is an argument to be made in favor of making use of this ETF to conveniently adjust one's exposure to silver prices, and then exit and opt for buying physical silver with the proceeds.

A Brief Overview of My 2017 Article on Industrial Silver Demand

The article I wrote in 2017, entitled " Not Enough Silver To Power The World Even If Solar Power Efficiency Were To Quadruple," was meant to highlight how much silver we have available to satisfy the rising trend of using it in industrial applications, such as solar power, high-performance electronic circuits, and so on. I used the hypothetical model of having to run the world exclusively on solar power to help visualize what we can and cannot do given the geological endowment we live within.

I included hypothetical efficiency gains to make the exercise more realistic. The conclusion that we do not have enough silver to power the world on solar is not entirely consequential, because we do have other sources of energy, such as solar, hydro, nuclear, and, of course, hydrocarbons, that continue to dominate the global energy market. It does help to highlight the fact that we could potentially use up all the silver on the planet in industrial applications.

The importance of my inclusion of assumed efficiency gains into the calculations is not only to measure potential demand growth more accurately, but also to highlight the fact that the price of silver might not necessarily produce the demand destruction that is assumed to occur in certain industries, if the price of silver surges as it just did in the past year. While a significant price increase in any input will create some demand destruction pressures, the efficiency gains that were once cited as a reason to be bearish on silver prices are now dulling the demand destruction effect that higher silver prices are likely to have on industrial demand.

Silver's High Conductivity Properties Are Irreplaceable in a World of High Efficiency.

According to the Silver Institute, in 2024, silver demand in solar panels reached 198 million ounces, while demand stagnated in 2025. It was just under 100 million ounces in 2017, when I highlighted the demand potential for this particular industrial application for silver.

Total silver demand in electronics and electrical applications was 466 million ounces, meaning that solar panels accounted for about 40% of total demand in these fields. The non-photovoltaics demand came from applications that require relatively small amounts of silver, such as in smartphones, that use anywhere between 0.1 grams to 0.35 grams per unit. Just to get a sense of how silver prices might affect demand from smartphones, one gram of silver currently costs less than $3; therefore, most smartphones currently use less than $1 worth of silver. Laptops use about a gram of silver, making it a minor cost factor.

About 20 grams of silver is used in a solar panel, which may account for about 15% to 30% of the total cost of manufacturing, based on recent estimates. This is the one industry where I do see some potential for demand destruction as companies find solutions to use less, in response to the rising cost of silver. However, rising demand for solar power may negate the efficiency gains that companies are coming up with and implementing.

The drive to achieve optimal performance in most new technologies has been a driving force behind industrial adoption of silver use, despite it being far more expensive compared with other conductive materials such as copper. At the same time, mined production has been on a slight declining path in the past decade.

Global mined silver supplies were about twice as high as silver demand for industrial use. As of last year, mined output was 19% higher compared with industrial demand for the metal.

It is important to understand this aspect of the silver market dynamic. Most of the silver used in industrial applications tends not to be recovered. Once industrial demand surpasses mined production, we will enter a period where the net silver found above ground in various forms will start to decline. Its scarcity value is therefore set to increase, which suggests that the current silver market price is not only justified, but that we should expect further gains.

The path toward a market balance where mined silver output will not be enough to satisfy the world's growing appetite for the metal in industrial applications will not be linear. In other words, we should not base our timeline on the pace at which the gap narrowed since 2016. Market forces are likely to make the remaining gap harder to close. Higher silver prices should stimulate miners into investing more, while industrial consumers are likely to look for ways to save through more efficient use or outright full substitution where it is technically feasible.

Mined silver output growth potential is, in part, limited by the fact that silver is often produced as a byproduct of other metals, such as copper. In other words, if output in other industrial metals is not growing at a robust pace, neither will silver as a byproduct. There are mines where silver is the primary product, but those mines are increasingly grappling with lower-quality ores, and it is unclear whether higher prices might trigger interest in increasing output from limited resources, especially if miners believe that prices will trend higher from here. On the demand side, as I already illustrated, there are some industries where demand destruction can occur, such as the solar industry, but many industries, such as those in the electronics business, will not be affected by silver prices, to such an extent that it will erode their profitability, or not be able to pass on the costs to the consumer.

The Gold Rally May Not Be the Main Silver Price Catalyst.

It is often assumed that, due to silver's precious metal role, often referred to as the "poor man's gold", silver prices will move largely in the same direction as gold.

As the chart shows, the SLV ETF mostly moved in step with the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD) until more recently, when silver prices shot up dramatically, comparatively speaking.

This tells us that while there is a strong correlation between gold and silver prices, there are currently other silver-specific factors that are becoming the main drivers of the silver market. In other words, the gold market no longer leads the silver market. A decoupling occurred due to the silver market-specific fundamentals that I highlighted. It remains to be seen to what extent the two markets will remain decoupled and for how long. For now, silver is being pushed forward by the rising gold market, but it also benefits from the industrial demand factor, which, in my view, means it is set to outperform gold for the foreseeable future.

Investment Implications

Given the roughly 200% increase in the share price of the SLV ETF over the past year, the obvious dilemma arises as to whether one should consider taking profits. I have been asking myself this same question, carefully pondering the fundamentals. Looking back at my 2017 article, meant in part to illustrate the long-term industrial demand potential for silver, I do not see this as a market bubble, but rather as an adjustment to fundamental realities that have been building for a while. Global mined silver output declined by about 10% in the past decade, while industrial demand for silver increased by roughly 40%.

We are, in my view, less than a decade away from industrial demand for silver outpacing mined output permanently. In other words, from that point on, the total amount of silver stored in various forms, be it bricks or jewelry will start to decline, with most of it ending up being permanently lost in industrial processes. As the price of silver continues to rise, efforts will probably be made to retrieve more of it from electronic devices and other applications. We will still eventually see more silver consumed in industrial applications than miners will be able to produce, even after accounting for retrieval rates.

I last bought SLV shares at the end of December, taking advantage of a minor pullback in silver prices. I intend to continue adding more shares incrementally on any significant pullbacks, while being mindful of keeping my overall position at a gain level of over 100%. I am currently at about 140%. The way I see it, the odds of my overall SLV investment strategy losing money as long as I keep above that 100% level are low.

I am certain that we will see sizable pullbacks in silver prices in the coming months and years, which will rattle some investors, while others will see the opportunity. Given that the current mined silver output is largely absorbed by industry, I expect companies that require silver as an input to start stockpiling when the price declines. In other words, we may have permanently moved to a higher silver price level that became the new normal. Based on these considerations, I am in the camp that sees opportunity in a sizable pullback in silver prices, not among those who will see it as a risk.

Risks to my Thesis

There are plenty of potential pitfalls to my thesis. Silver demand can be curtailed due to several factors. The higher price might lead to a decline in jewelry demand and industrial demand through substitution. Higher prices may also spur higher mined output than expected. The supply/demand balance, especially when it comes to mined supply versus industrial demand, can be changed significantly, assuming that the higher price factor will have a higher impact than expected.

A return to central banks refocusing on shoring up the health and stability of fiat currency systems around the world might reverse the high demand for gold & silver as store of value mechanisms. I see very few indications that we are on the brink of sound fiat currency policies. Deficits around the world are rising, and central banks are accommodating. This, on its own, provides a higher price floor under silver prices in my view. The industrial demand situation is not likely to be reversed either.

Conclusions

The dramatic rise in the share price of the SLV ETF ordinarily would tend to raise questions on whether this may be the time to consider taking profits. In most cases, I tend to do just that, with questions on whether there may be more upside due to market momentum or other factors, leaving me holding just a minor position at most, while I tend to sell the bulk of such a lucrative investment.

In this particular case, the dramatic increase in share price is a reaction to a dramatic shift in fundamentals. We are clearly on our way to a silver market balance where industrial demand will surpass mined output, making the scarcity value dynamics of silver superior to that of gold, since most of the gold that is mined, adds to the volume of gold available above ground. We may be entering the era where the volume of silver above ground that is recognized and marketed as such will begin to shrink.

The concept I introduced in my article almost a decade ago, namely that there is more than enough potential industrial demand to consume all the known silver resources that the world was endowed with, above ground and below, is very relevant to the silver market situation today. It helps to illustrate the shift into a net deficit of mined output versus industrial demand dynamic, which will slowly start to devour the silver supplies that the world currently holds above ground to make up for the deficit.

I am not entirely certain when this might happen. We have come a lot closer to it in the past decade, and even though we are headed toward that shift at a slower pace now, due to higher silver prices slowing the convergence down, I believe there is a chance that we will hit that point as soon as the end of this decade. Within this context, silver prices are set to keep rising going forward, for the foreseeable future, making current silver investments a buy & hold, rather than an opportunity to take profits on the already sizable gains we experienced in the past year.

data.title: Why Is The U.S. Dollar So Strong To Start 2026? EUR/USD And Dollar Index Overview
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Despite what the banks and global analysts say in their 2026 projections, the dollar (DXY) has strengthened since the start of the year and shows only few signs of weakening.

Of course, we are just halfway through January; there is still plenty of time for things to change. However, the dollar's trajectory is now looking quite different.

The Dollar Index isn't at 110.00 like it was just a year ago, before the greenback corrected by 10% against all of its major peers - now hanging right around the 100.00 level.

Consolidating since mid-July, bears have mostly barked, but the harsh descent seems already over.

The reason for that? US economic performance is still beating its competitors despite tariffs, and US firms, particularly in the tech and AI sectors, are dominant.

Despite fears and headlines, American employment is still in a decent spot and has stalled its expected decline, as reflected in recent jobless claims and non-farm payroll numbers.

To add injury to insult, the US Federal Funds Rate is the highest among the majors (except for the Bank of England's Rate, with which it is tied), leaving basis trades well active.

It wasn't just the greenback that was targeted last year - currency debasement hit all majors, as seen in gold's performance against the OECD FX basket.

So, is the dollar still in danger? Yes.

Policy directives from the Trump Administration can be unpredictable for nations and investors - look at Canada PM Mark Carney turning to China for a Trade Deal in the absence of such from the US.

And of course, the Fed's Independence at stake keeps hurting the buck's prospects, notably fueling rallies in metals since August, but hasn't shown much change since, even with the latest Powell investigation from the DOJ.

But as long as commodities are still priced in US dollars, banks and firms still require dollar funding, and the US Treasury is still the most liquid, safe haven asset available, the dollar can't just disappear from the system - leading to the current market conditions.

Finally, with the recent interventions in Venezuela, threats to Iran, the dollar saw demand from Freedom Trade flows.

On the other hand, some technical signals could point to a short-term correction in USD demand.

We will look at the Dollar Index and EUR/USD to assess the current state of the market and whether more upside is warranted for the dollar after its strong, surprising start to 2026.

Dollar Index Daily Chart

The US dollar is rallying quite strongly since breaking out of its descending sequence from November.

Rebounding in a very consistent uptrend, DXY has held support and shown no corrections since December 24.

However, now reaching the highs of its 99.50 resistance zone, momentum is weakening, as can be seen on the Daily RSI.

Let's see what will tilt the scales on the 4H chart.

4H Chart and Levels of Interest

Showing a bear divergence in the 4H time frame RSI, combined with the reaction to the resistance level, it looks like the dollar is losing some steam.

Still, keep a close eye on the confluence of the 4H 50-MA at 98.96 and the 2026 channel lows.

  • Breaking that level would point to continued downside and could retest the December 2025 lows at 97.75.
  • In the event of any intervention in Iran, the dollar can also spike above, so keep that in mind.

Overall, the moving averages are flatlining in the US dollar, indicating further consolidation/range-bound action for the time being.

Levels of interest for the Dollar Index:

Resistance Levels

  • Immediate resistance 99.25-99.50
  • 2026 highs 99.49
  • 100.00 to 100.50 main resistance zone
  • 100.376 November highs

Support Levels

  • 98.96 4H 50-MA and channel lows
  • 98.50-98.80 intraday pivot zone
  • 98.00 key support (+/- 100 pips)
  • December lows 97.75
  • 97.40-97.80 August range support
  • 2025 lows 96.40-96.80 support

EUR/USD 4H chart and Technical Levels

EUR/USD is showing reactions corroborating with the slowing momentum in the DXY.

Bouncing from the 1.16 support and channel lows, mean reversion should hold well towards the 4H 50-MA at 1.1655.

Rallying on high volume and candles could easily lead to a breakout of the descending channel towards the flatlining 200-period moving average (at 1.17).

Levels to place on your EUR/USD charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 1.1630-1.1670 Pivot zone (channel highs and 4H 50-MA)
  • 1.17 4H 200-MA
  • 1.1750 mini-resistance
  • Resistance Zone around 1.18 (+/- 150 pips)

Support Levels

  • 1.1580-1.16 key support
  • 1.1560 channel lows
  • 1.1470-1.15 pivotal support
  • 1.1350-1.14 support
  • Session lows 1.1593

Safe Trades, and keep a close eye on weekend risk and headlines!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Recursion Pharmaceuticals: Stock Unresponsive To Clinical Updates
data.description:
Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX) provided clinical updates from two of its programs in late 2025, but the company's stock hasn't mounted a sustained rally. In my previous article in August 2025, I rated the stock a sell, noting that while there were two readouts ahead, updates might not allay concerns about the company's pipeline and the valuation allowed room for the stock to fall. This article looks at where RXRX stands now, and the readouts ahead for the company.

Updated data from the CDK7 inhibitor REC-617

RXRX's Q3'25 earnings in November came with an update on the company's CDK7 inhibitor, REC-617, which is being tested in various solid tumor types in a trial called ELUCIDATE. It might be hard to judge the market's impression of the data since the update came at the same time as other updates.

Firstly, it is worth noting that the prior update on ELUCIDATE in a December 2024 press release noted REC-617 had produced one partial response in a platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (PROC) patient, and four cases of stable disease, from 18 evaluable patients (19 had been enrolled at that time, or at the time of a November 2024 data cutoff). That 2024 update noted no maximum tolerated dose had been determined, despite doses up to 20 mg once daily.

By comparison the November 2025 update on ELUCIDATE comes from a September 29, 2025, data cutoff, at which time the company had dosed 29 patients. Only 10 additional patients of data isn't a huge update. Further, the company now notes a maximum tolerated dose of 10 mg once daily, and there doesn't seem to have been additional partial responses, although the company now notes five cases, rather than four, of stable disease. That being said, RXRX's 2024 press release did note that efficacy wasn't expected as monotherapy, and RXRX does point out it is seeing reduced GI toxicity compared to a previous CDK7 inhibitor, samuraciclib. Also on the safety front, the company noted there have been no grade 4 or 5 treatment-related adverse events reported.

The update isn't a complete let down then, but it does mean there will be additional waiting to see what REC-617 can really do. ELUCIDATE is continuing into a phase 2 monotherapy study in PROC patients and combination work (with bevacizumab plus chemotherapy) in the phase 1 portion has also begun. RXRX's January 2026 presentation notes early safety and pharmacokinetic data is expected from work with REC-617 in H1'27 (slide 31).

Updated data from TUPELO

REC-4881 is RXRX's MEK1/2 inhibitor which the company is developing for familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP). In May 2025, the company reported an update showing efficacy data from six patients treated with the drug, in which five patients saw a reduction in polyp burden, but unfortunately one patient saw a large increase in polyp burden.

I was fairly negative on REC-4881 at the time, although the December 2025 update looks better, in my opinion. The updated data from TUPELO include 12 efficacy evaluable patients treated with 4 mg of REC-4881, which importantly suggest there may be some durability to the reduction in polyp burden seen following REC-4881 treatment.

There are a few notes, however. RXRX discussed the updated TUPELO data in December 2025 on a conference call, and noted regarding the patient who previously didn't do well in the May 2025 update, that the baseline measurement of polyp burden was effectively suboptimal. It appears this patient now, rather than having a 595% increase in polyp burden, is only reported as having a 32% increase in polyp burden after 12 weeks of treatment with REC-4881.

As you may recall from our previous presentation in May, we had one unusual result with a nonresponder. Following that data cut and as described in the footnote 1 on the slide, we did a quality review and identified that this patient had a suboptimal bowel prep at baseline. So to ensure for an accurate and like-for-like assessment, polyp burden was reevaluated using video review restricted to the clean regions of the GI tract at all time points, so baseline, week 13 and week 25.

Elizabeth Bruckheimer, Vice President of Clinical Development at RXRX, from the earnings call on December 8, 2025, discussion of updated TUPELO data.

Also, while the durability data are generally positive, one non-responder did have a larger increase in polyp burden, although according to the footnotes in RXRX's December presentation of updated TUPELO data, that is from a single polyp growing.

The safety data from 19 efficacy evaluable patients treated with 4 mg REC-4881 includes three grade 3 adverse events, but no grade 4 or 5 treatment-related adverse events.

The market had little reaction to the TUPELO update however. One concern might simply be that further optimization of the ideal REC-4881 regime, and a much larger trial, might be needed to see if the company can convert the reduction in polyp burden into a reduction in the need for further surgeries or progression to cancer. RXRX plans to discuss the data with the FDA in H1'26, and an update from those discussions, providing clarity on registrational clinical work, represents a near-term catalyst for the stock.

Financial Overview

RXRX finished Q3'25 with $667.1M in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash, although with Q3'25 earnings the company noted cash of ~$785M at October 9, 2025, aided by use of RXRX's At-the-Market facility. More recently, with a presentation at the JP Morgan Healthcare Conference, RXRX noted $755M in YE 2025 cash, and that it expected runway through year-end 2027 (slide 31). For Q3'25, RXRX reported, revenue of $5.2M, cost of revenue of $14.7M, R&D expenses of $121.1M, G&A expenses of $41.6M and a net loss of $162.3M.

RXRX's net cash used in operating activities was $325.7M in the first nine months of 2025. Even considering the cash raised following the end of the quarter, and taking pro forma cash as $785M at the end of Q3'25, RXRX would be out of cash in 22 months at the current rate of burn, or around mid 2027. That being said, RXRX is projecting cash burn below $390M in 2026 (slide 31, JP Morgan presentation), and that number excludes partnership inflows. For example, in Q4'25, the company brought in a $30M milestone from Genentech and Roche Holding AG (RHHBY), which brought RXRX to $500M in inflows from upfront and milestone payments from its partners to date. As such, even though inflows from these partnerships are irregular, it is plausible that RXRX might be able to bring in further payments to help it reach its cash runway guidance should cash burn accelerate.

RXRX trades with a market cap of $2.5B ($4.76 per share).

Risks

Although this article doesn't advocate for a short in RXRX, some risks of a short position will be considered here, given the sell rating.

Firstly, RXRX is set to report some data from a trial of REC-1245, a RBM39 degrader, in solid tumors and lymphoma. It is very hard to predict how the drug will perform given this will be the first readout from that trial. If data impresses, the stock could rally.

Secondly, RXRX could announce positive feedback from its discussions with the FDA regarding development of REC-4881 in FAP. For example, if the FDA is open to a registrational trial with an endpoint based on polyp reduction or Spigelman stage, rather than endpoints that might require larger or longer trials, then the stock could rally.

Lastly, RXRX could announce additional milestones from its existing partnerships, or new partnerships which could bring in upfront payments. The former scenario might not provide much of a reaction if the milestone is modest, but a whole new partnership could certainly send the stock on a run.

Conclusions and rating

RXRX has produced data from a handful more of patients in its REC-617 trial ELUCIDATE, and similarly a bit more data from its TUPELO trial of REC-4881. Given the amount of money the company has burned through in the time, I'm somewhat unimpressed. Of course the company burns through money doing work for its partners, and that does bring in cash, but I would've liked to have seen some more data from its trials of its own drugs. That being said, RXRX's REC-4881 has potential in FAP and I do see the data update as positive. Unfortunately, I believe there could still be a sizable or long registrational trial ahead of it, and getting underway with that work could still take a while. I think it would have been ideal to have a larger cohort of patients test various regimes (eg. 12 weeks on, 12 weeks off REC-4881) and follow them for a year or two before moving to a registrational trial.

Regarding REC-617, it looks like additional data won't be seen until 2027, although I do think it is worth keeping an eye on. As for REC-1245, the RBM39 degrader, with no data to analyze in humans so far, it seems difficult to predict how that drug might perform.

RXRX also still trades well above cash, and is burning cash, although it did make good use of its ATM to exploit its elevated market cap. I don't think RXRX is as good a short now as previously, because I can't predict the outcome of the upcoming REC-1245 data, nor the FDA regulatory discussions, which are near-term events plus the cash RXRX has is sufficient for a while. I still find it hard to rate RXRX a hold given how far it trades above cash, its cash burn, the fact that it produces data from its own programs at a modest rate, and while the data looks interesting the ultimate fate of REC-4881 and REC-617 is hard to judge at such an early stage. Therefore, I still rate RXRX a sell.

data.title: The Weakest Links Within The AI Bubble
data.description:
The equity markets have largely muddled along in the first two weeks of 2026, which has largely consisted of directionless trading to this point. This comes after three-plus years of outsized gains from the major indexes. Most of this performance has been powered by the Magnificent Seven, which have ridden the AI Revolution wave to prosperity. The largest 10 equities now account for just over 40% of overall market capitalization. A concentration not seen since the tail end of the Internet Boom.

Hopefully, 2026 is the year the rally broadens, driven by increasing profit growth from the S&P 493. Will the AI Narrative remain intact in 2026? I penned an article earlier this week on the potential triggers for the eventual popping of the AI Bubble. Whether that happens in 2026, 2027, or even 2028 is an unknown.

In today's column, I focus on the weakest links within the AI narrative that has driven the lion's share of equity gains since ChatGPT debuted in November 2022.

NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) has become the linchpin for the AI Revolution. The stock is up 11-fold since ChatGPT became available to the masses.

Does NVIDIA deserve a market cap that is roughly the annual GDP of Germany, the largest economy in Europe? Probably not over the long term. Will this primarily hardware-focused company be able to maintain 70% plus margins over the long run? No company in history has been able to accomplish this.

However, given its breakneck growth, NVDA trading at around 40 times forward earnings is not outlandish. Especially given the company's growing cash holdings after the company had nearly $61 billion in free cash flow in FY2025. Recent results and analyst commentary around chip makers Sandisk Corporation (SNDK), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) show the chip sellers are firmly in control of the semiconductor market.

RBC Capital Markets came out with a forecast on Thursday that projects semiconductor revenue related to artificial intelligence applications to grow from $220 billion in 2025 to more than $550 billion by 2028.

So, what are potentially the weaker players that could eventually undermine the AI narrative and be the first to see their stock prices crack? Here are several names that make my list.

Obviously, OpenAI has to be at/near the top of a list like this. The company is a core part of the entire AI ecosystem. OpenAI has made over $1.4 trillion in commitments for future computing power. That is more than 100 times its likely FY2025 revenue, and the company is bleeding cash. However, OpenAI is not a public company (yet), and I covered OpenAI extensively in this article from December. Therefore, we will leave it at that for this column.

It is difficult to envision a list of potentially overvalued AI equities without having Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) highlighted. The company builds and deploys software platforms that can be used to enable users to identify patterns hidden deep within datasets. The company's capabilities can also utilize commercial large language models (LLMs), which can transform structured and unstructured data into LLM-understandable objects. Palantir's offerings have been made to various intelligence agencies to assist in counterterrorism investigations and operations. Some have accused the company as a primary driver of what they see as the evolving surveillance state.

The stock is up some 170% over the past 12 months, driven by impressive sales growth that the analyst community sees growing at roughly a 40% CAGR over the next five years.

That said, the stock has rallied to uncharted territory. The stock trades at over 175 times forward earnings and roughly 95 times revenues. In contrast, the price-to-sales on the NASDAQ is just over seven times, which itself seems more than stretched from a historical basis.

Up next is Meta Platforms (META), which is dramatically ramping up its capex budget. The company recently narrowed its FY2025 capex guidance to between $70 billion and $72 billion in FY2025, from $66 billion to $72 billion previously. This is up sharply from under $40 billion in FY2024.

It is also important to remember that unlike Alphabet (GOOG) and Microsoft (MSFT), Meta doesn’t have a cloud service or a clear revenue stream that’s tied to its massive and growing AI investments. Company management has stated its AI investment will benefit its core digital ads business as the result from better targeting.

One must keep in mind this is a company whose Reality Labs has racked up over $70 billion in cumulative losses, driving Meta Platforms forays into Virtual Reality and the Metaverse, with little to show for this large investment. The company is now laying off some 1,500 employees from this division as it focuses now on its AI buildout. What possibly could go wrong?

We end with Oracle (ORCL). The stock does not have the extreme valuations of Palantir trading at around 26 times forward earnings or the lack of clear AI-related revenue streams like Meta Platforms. What Oracle does have is a considerable and growing debt load. The company is spending tens of billions of dollars to build out AI computing capacity. A good chunk of this is connected to the huge $300 billion deal it inked with OpenAI this summer. And the capacity from this deal will need the electrical output from two Hoover Dams to operate.

Oracle is taking on more debt to fund this buildout. Concerns around this rising leverage have credit default swaps, or CDS, on Oracle's now over $100 billion of debt at their highest levels since the Great Financial Crisis. The stock was hit briefly when Blue Owl (OWL) walked away from a potential $10 billion financing deal connected with an Oracle data center in Michigan in mid-December. The stock has fully recovered from that drop, it should be noted, and I just posted an article about Blue Owl.

It was disclosed this week that a proposed class action lawsuit has been filed in a Manhattan court. This is on behalf of investors who purchased $18 billion worth of debt issued by Oracle in September. This triggered a 5% hit to the shares and highlights the increasing investor worries about Oracle's balance sheet.

Every major technology paradigm shift in the United States, from the building of railroads, electrification, and the birth of the internet, has spawned a market bubble that eventually popped. There is no reason the current paradigm shift around AI will not follow the same trajectory.

Whether valuation metrics can continue to be historically stretched or the AI bubble implodes in 2026 or 2027 is anybody's guess. That said, when the AI narrative starts to deflate, one or more of the tech giants listed above are likely to be in the vanguard of that future bear market.

data.title: It's More Complicated Than Mag Seven
data.description:
Since we are now sixteen days into the year 2026 of the Common Era, it is once again time for everyone in the financial field to make bold pronouncements about the Great Rotation that is to January what the Great Pumpkin of Peanuts fame is to October (i.e., the wild imagining of an impressionable mind). It’s true that small-cap and value stocks have done comparatively well in this year’s early days. And as of this morning, five of the fabled “Magnificent Seven” stocks that for the past three years have served as an easy market shorthand for “AI leaders” are trailing the S&P 500. But the way we see it, this is less about the eclipse of the AI narrative, which shows no signs of going away as a core component of US economic growth, than it is about the AI story getting more complicated, with more careful parsing of where different companies actually stack up in the narrative. We’ll introduce two names in the chart below that are not part of the Mag Seven but are very much at the center of all things AI: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSMC)(TSM) and Micron Technology (MU).

What’s In a Name

In the chart above, we see outsized gains for the year so far by Micron and TSMC, up 18 percent and 12 percent, respectively. Among the Mag 7, only Alphabet (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN) are ahead of the index, while Nvidia (NVDA) is just slightly behind and the other four (Tesla (TSLA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL) and Meta (META)) trail by a wider margin. This week TSMC, which essentially manufactures all the chips that other companies design, reported another quarter of blowout earnings, growing revenue by more than 20 percent, expanding gross and operating profit margins, and raising its forward guidance by more than the consensus of sell-side analysts had expected (and they had expected a lot – the performance bar for TSMC was high). In terms of the potential impact on overall economic growth, TSMC’s capital expenditure forecast was also raised to $52-56 billion for this year alone, with more to follow. Translation: the AI infrastructure spending binge that has been the central economic story for the past three years is not slowing down. For this single reason, let alone other factors like consumer spending or net exports, we believe it remains unlikely that we will see a recession in the US this year.

One big part of that infrastructure spending binge will be on memory, in the form of DRAM chips, and that is where Micron Technology comes into the story. Micron has been around for a very long time, and its core competency for all this time has been the comparatively dull memory segment of the chip market. It turns out, though, that the memory capabilities of Micron’s DRAM chips are essential to AI model development, and currently demand is outpacing supply. Micron is investing heavily to meet that increased demand – hence, yes, more spending that will translate into the GDP growth equation.

Tell Me Your Story

We focused on these two companies (there are others) because they reflect a general sharpening in the AI story that, we think, will lead to more dispersion among the performance of stocks in this space, rather than the simplistic “buy Mag Seven” story of yesteryear. Tesla, Apple, and Meta are the names we think could be on the other end of this dispersion (though any one or all of them could perform well for other reasons – none of them are AI pure plays in any sense). As for Nvidia, which has been the big name in this space ever since ChatGPT mania took off in early 2023, it continues to maintain a solid competitive advantage in supplying the leading edge of graphic processing unit (GPU) chips to AI developers. But competition from China, while not fully formed today, will be a competitive threat the company must take into account as it charts the next phase of its strategy.

Back to the issue we raised back at the beginning of this piece, about the purported Great Rotation from the things that have done well recently to the undervalued corners of the market among value and small-cap names. Will this happen, or will it fizzle out as these things often do? We don’t know, nor are we inclined to shuffle around a lot and run up big capital gains in order to find out. We are less interested in momentum-driven rotations and more interested in having a better understanding of the landscape in that part of the market situated at the center of economic growth. For now, at least, that remains the dynamic in the constantly changing AI space.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Market Faces Renewed Liquidity Pressure
data.description:
Liquidity conditions in the market are likely to tighten this coming week as the U.S. Treasury increases its bill issuance. The increased issuance of bills should push the Treasury General Account higher, which serves as the U.S. Government's checking account, draining reserves from the banking system even as the Fed buys Treasury bills.

Additionally, margin debt in the equity market continues to rise; however, the growth rate appears to be slowing. Historically, when reserves have declined, leverage growth has also declined. This suggests that tightening conditions seen in the fourth quarter are already starting to have an impact.

Reserves Rises

This past week, reserve balances held at the Fed increased to $3.06 trillion, while the Treasury General Account fell to around $780 billion. The rise in reserves over the past couple of weeks is partially explained by the decline in the TGA and by the Fed's increase in the asset side of the balance sheet through Treasury bill purchases, as announced at the December FOMC meeting. Overall, reserves are down sharply from their July highs of $3.4 trillion.

Over the next month, the NY Fed plans to buy about $55 billion in Treasury bills. However, this will not likely offset the potential size of the increase in the TGA from Treasury bill issuance. The TGA has a quarter-end target of $850 billion, and if net issuance increases, it is possible the TGA will reach that target sooner, potentially even before the quarterly refunding announcement at the end of January. As a result, the Fed's balance sheet is likely to remain steady or contract in the near term.

Signs of Tightening

It is easy to identify whether the increase in Treasury issuance is tightening liquidity conditions. The Secured Overnight Financing Rate would begin to rise, as seen during the fourth quarter, when SOFR surged above the Effective Fed Funds Rate. The spread between the two interest rate measures should be negative, since SOFR transactions are secured, whereas the effective Fed Funds Rate transactions are not. But when funding pressures increased in the fall, SOFR rose sharply, widening spreads.

Additionally, if SOFR surges above the rate the Fed charges on the standing repo facility of 3.75%, it is likely that standing repo facility usage will increase. During the fall, the repo facility was used mostly around Treasury settlement dates, with the heaviest usage at month-end, when coupon settlements were larger.

Margin Growth Slows

Data from FINRA reported that equity margin balances grew to over $1.2 trillion. But more importantly, the year-over-year growth rate in margin balances appears to have slowed, mirroring the rise and fall of liquidity held at the Fed. The recent decline in the Fed's liquidity appears to be slowing the growth of leverage.

Changes in leverage have also been strongly correlated with changes in the S&P 500. This could explain why the S&P 500 is starting to struggle, having traded virtually sideways since peaking in late October, while the NASDAQ 100 is lower.

Overall, the Fed's efforts to stabilize the balance sheet will take some time to develop. Therefore, in the near term, liquidity conditions are more likely to tighten than to ease.

data.title: BioAge Labs: Why Getting Into The Brain Might Be A Headache For Investors
data.description:
BioAge Labs' (BIOA) recent 3-month rally appears inspired by two events.

In December, the company reported interim Phase 1 findings in which its oral small molecule NLRP3 inhibitor, BGE-102, reduced markers of inflammation.

Soon following Phase 1 data, Eli Lilly (LLY) announced its intention to acquire Ventyx for its NLRP3 inhibitors, VTX2735 and VTX3232, among other anti-inflammatory investigational assets.

In the article that follows, I explore whether BIOA is worth pursuing after an NLRP3 inhibitor-inspired rally.

NLRP3 Inhibitors: Past, Present, & Future

NLRP3 has been explored in the past. However, liver safety signals have stopped early programs, such as MCC950 and Roche’s (RHHBY) GDC-2394. So, for oral chronic use indications such as cardiometabolic or neurodegeneration, the safety bar is high.

Both BioAge’s and Ventyx’s drugs were designed with the past limitations of NLRP3 inhibition in mind. Now, BGE-102 is a bit different from VTX2735 in that BGE-102 penetrates the brain.

In obese patients with elevated baseline inflammation, BGE-102 generated an 86% median hsCRP reduction at Day 14 at 120 mg every day. This coincided with reductions in IL-6 and fibrinogen.

These results are much different from older programs that often demonstrated either acute anti-inflammatory efficacy only at very high doses or early PD that was derailed by liver toxicity.

Prior to its acquisition by Eli Lilly, Ventyx’s plan was to evaluate VTX2735 in cardiovascular diseases with an initial focus on secondary prevention of MACE and recurrent pericarditis.

Now, plans for its brain-penetrant NLRP3 inhibitor, VTX3232, appear less concrete following Phase 2 data in both obesity with cardiovascular risk factors (“no effect on weight either as monotherapy or as an add-on to semaglutide”) and Parkinson's.

BioAge, too, is pursuing cardiovascular risk factors and obesity. The planned Phase 2a study (see Slide 35) will evaluate the percentage change in hsCRP as the primary endpoint over 12 weeks. This begs the question, what are the implications of exploring a brain-penetrant NLRP3 inhibitor in obesity and cardiovascular disease, rather than neurodegenerative diseases?

To me, this seems like a disadvantage for BioAge. Obesity and cardiovascular risk reduction are large chronic populations. So safety signals are much less tolerated by regulators, prescribers, and payers than they would be in severe neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson's. In this context, brain penetration is a safety and monitoring burden. So I'm having a difficult time understanding why BioAge is opting for indications of systemic inflammation rather than pursuing a CNS indication. Think of it this way: if peripheral NLRP3 inhibition is what is actually required to reduce markers like hsCRP, what good is it to have the drug active in the brain?

BioAge counters by saying that it views obesity itself as a brain-linked NLRP3 biology problem. Of course, we already know the “obesity-brain” link is true to some extent, given that GLP-1s also exert activity within the brain. And we do have preclinical evidence that NLRP3 inhibition can drive weight loss in diet-induced obesity mouse models.

That said, the question remains whether CNS penetration adds human benefit beyond what peripheral NLRP3 inhibition could achieve itself. And in BioAge's upcoming Phase 2 trial, the near-term clinical plan is a 12-week hsCRP-driven program. Here, CNS exposure is not required to move hsCRP (judging by Ventyx's VTX2735 data). It seems to merely expand the safety and tolerability burden in a large chronic prevention population. So BioAge is tasked with showing a clinically meaningful delta that possibly depends on CNS activity, such as superior weight loss magnitude, durability, or reduced rebound after stopping GLP-1 therapy.

So I think the lesson here is that brain penetration can be both a differentiation and a burden. While it may broaden optionality, it also raises the bar for long-duration safety monitoring, especially if the initial commercial ambition is primary care cardiovascular risk. And of course, whether or not further hsCRP reductions and related biomarkers on top of the existing standard of care will procure meaningful additive clinical benefits is a major unknown. That said, Eli Lilly's own interest in this mechanism of action does help validate it. But we also must be aware that Eli Lilly is merely making a calculated bet and has a peripherally-restricted option. No one knows for sure how it'll work out until the data is all there.

Pipeline & Platform

BioAge is not a one-trick pony. It also has some APJ agonist programs as well as discovery platforms and partnerships with large pharma companies like Novartis (NVS) and Eli Lilly.

BioAge had to discontinue one of its own APJ agonists, azelaprag, after it showed liver enzyme elevations without a clear dose dependence. Now, the company is framing the next wave of its APJ work as a nanobody plus new oral small molecules. So they are attempting to preserve the target thesis while changing the molecule class to reduce idiosyncratic risk.

While partnerships can help validate the discovery platform and produce long-term value, the near-term value creation is clearly dominated by BGE-102 clinical execution. Especially because its partner targets are not publicly disclosed clinical assets yet.

Financial Health

At the end of the third quarter of 2025, BioAge had $295.9 million in cash and short-term investments. BioAge does not have any significant debt obligations.

In the trailing 12 months, BioAge utilized $58.5 million in cash from operations. This is often used as a proxy for cash burn. After doing some back-of-the-napkin math, it appears that BioAge has roughly 4 years of cash runway. This is consistent with the company's own cash runway guidance. It believes that its existing cash and marketable securities will be enough to fund operations through 2029.

Final Words

I wouldn't be quick to buy its stock into this rally (initiate “Hold” rating). Despite having multiple programs, the only disclosed human efficacy at present is short-duration biomarker movement for BGE-102. Moving forward, BioAge’s valuation sensitivity will likely be dominated by whether or not BGE-102 can safely reproduce large hsCRP and IL-6 effects over 12 weeks across more dose cohorts. Of course, despite initially pursuing obesity, if it becomes evident that its drug doesn't confer additional weight loss, the company can always shift its focus to cardiovascular risk reduction. But if that turns out to be the case, the company will have a harder time justifying the brain penetration's additive benefit.

So all in all, I'm feeling a bit mixed on the stock. Eli Lilly's ~$1.2 billion acquisition of Ventyx does help validate the potential of this mechanism of action, and BioAge does have human data. On the flip side, perhaps there is a good reason that Eli Lilly opted for the NLRP3 optionality (brain-penetrant and peripherally-restricted).

data.title: Next Week Could Be Brutal, Major Correction Approaching
data.description:
On the brink of a major correction

The stock market started the new year with the rotation from tech (QQQ) to value, specifically small caps (IWM), cyclical large caps (DIA), and even the emerging markets (EEM).

The unfolding thesis is that tech stocks are now overvalued, while the AI theme is slowly fading, while growth is expected to pick up, which is resulting in a broadening of the rally.

However, in my opinion, this is the first phase of the AI bubble burst, while the economy is actually going to slow this year, with a possible recession. As the tech sector technically weakens, the sentiment will likely deteriorate, and the broad market is likely to sink together with the tech sector.

So, there are two charts to start the analysis:

First, the Shiller PE ratio is near 41, and that means that the S&P 500 (SP500) is a major bubble, comparable only to the 2000 dot-com bubble. This chart has been shared numerous times, and yet, nobody is really taking it seriously. Investors think that there has to be a trigger to burst the bubble.

The second chart is the Nasdaq 100 futures (NDX). Technically, the Nasdaq 100 is facing a major breakdown at the 100dma moving average. The price pattern looks bearish, where the series of lower highs created a bearish flag, with the rising 100dma resistance. Once the 100dma resistance is broken, a major breakdown is likely.

The bubble in the S&P 500 is due to the tech mega-caps, and the technically weak Nasdaq 100 chart is a sign of rotation out of tech. Investors are rotating out of tech because AI is a credit-driven infrastructure bubble, likely in the first stage of the burst.

The question is what will trigger the Nasdaq 100 breakdown—and a major correction in a broader stock market.

Next week has several potential triggers.

The Supreme Court decision on tariffs

The SCOTUS has already passed two opportunities to make the decision on IERPA tariffs. Next week the Supreme Court has scheduled two opinion days, on Tuesday and Wednesday.

Thus, it's very likely that the decision on the legality of Trump tariffs will come next week. Why is this important?

Trump's economic plan is heavily dependent on tariffs, and thus, if SCOTUS rules the tariffs illegal, the entire economic plan collapses.

Basically, Trump's economic plan is a revival of US manufacturing, and the key to this plan is the foreign direct investment in the US. That means foreign companies are expected to open their factories in the US, which is expected to boost the US labor market.

The tariffs are the primary tool to “force” foreign companies to invest in the US, and the trade negotiations after the Liberation Day resulted in trillions of dollars of committed foreign investment in the US.

If SCOTUS declares IEEPA tariffs illegal, the Trump administration will have to reimpose tariffs using different statutes, but this also means it will have to renegotiate trade deals, which means the foreign direct investment will be delayed or canceled. As a result, the labor market is likely to continue to weaken and ultimately result in a recession.

In addition, if the US Treasury is forced to refund the already collected tariffs, the bond market could react with rising 10Y yields (US10Y), and this could also trigger a correction.

In summary, the SCOTUS decision will likely result in macro uncertainty—and the decision is likely next week.

Geopolitics with a focus on Iran

The situation in Iran escalated this week, and there is a high chance of a regional conflict that could cause a spike in the price of oil (USO).

Basically, this is likely the last opportunity to cause a regime change in Iran, which would be a boost to the region and also the global economy. Iranian people are protesting against the regime, and the Trump administration has vowed to protect the protesters. Iran has vowed to defend itself and attack the regional US bases if attacked. Given that this is a specific attack on the Iranian regime, it is more likely to resort to desperate measures and attack the regional oil infrastructure. Thus, the oil price spike is likely—and this could also trigger a correction.

The current status of the situation is that Trump deescalated, acknowledging that the regime has stopped with executions, but at the same time, he redirected US Lincoln to the Middle East. An inflationary shock due to the spike in oil is the last thing Trump wants before the midterms, but at the same time, the opportunity to change the regime in Iran will be difficult to pass on. I expect that there will be limited strikes next week, as the US Lincoln arrives in the area.

Affordability and the Fed's independence

Other risks are rising, which could also trigger a correction next week.

President Trump is taking the affordability issue seriously leading to the midterms. However, the affordability policies are not necessarily market friendly. The cap on credit card interest rates to 10%, limiting instructional investment in residential housing, and limits on buybacks and dividends are examples of affordability policies that are not market friendly. I expect more announcements of similar affordability policies next week.

President Trump could also announce the new Fed Chair, and if it's Kevin Hassett, the bond market could react negatively, which could also trigger a correction.

Implications

The risks are rising, while the technicals look vulnerable. Thus, we are likely near a major correction, which could be triggered next week.

A serious correction needs either a recessionary trigger or an inflationary trigger.

The SCOTUS decision against IEEPA tariffs could be recessionary (pointing to a weakening labor market due to delayed or canceled DFI) and inflationary (refunds to widen the fiscal deficit). The spike in oil prices due to Iran's escalation would be inflationary. Thus, both of these events are capable of triggering a correction.

Ultimately, the AI bubble is likely to burst as the major AI stocks report earnings. There will be a lot of questions about the AI capex. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) CEO made a comment at the conference call: “You’re trying to ask us whether AI demand is real or not. I’m also very nervous about it.”

My prediction for 2026 is still a recessionary bear market with the AI bubble burst, and it could be triggered next week.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rose's Income Garden 2025 Investment Changes: 21 Sales And 14 Adds
data.description:
Rose’s Income Garden “RIG” portfolio had a net decrease of 7 investments for a total of 77 to end 2025. That decrease came from selling 21 and buying 14 investments, which is quite the movement. The forward yield with all the changes has remained consistent at 6.3%. That yield is a goal of my management to replace yield and income along with retaining the same sector defensive weighting. RIG goal is to have a minimum 50% of income coming from the defensive sectors and individual defensive stocks.

This is just a quick overview of 2025 that offers some insights into those sector weightings and the why of all those changes.

Sectors

RIG owns investments in all 11 sectors as listed below, which are used for the weightings and defensive positioning. I have indicated those "I consider defensive" even though others may not.

Defense:

1- Consumer Staples.

2- Healthcare.

3- Communication - I consider defensive.

4- Utility.

5- Information Technology - I consider defensive.

Non Defensive:

Some individual stocks are considered defensive even though the sector may not.

6- Consumer Discretionary.

7- Industrial - I consider defensive aerospace and defense stocks.

8- Energy.

9- Material.

10- Real Estate - healthcare I consider defensive.

11- Financial.

-preferred- I consider defensive.

-bonds- I consider defensive.

-BDCs, cryptocurrency and others.

This article will reveal the sold and added stocks in relation to the sectors they reside in.

The reasons they were sold will also be listed and remain reasons that I will continue to use for my investing.

Selling Reasons

Some companies may possibly fit in more than one category.

Low or no dividend growth: HD, ATT, CVS, PFE, CSCO

Dividend Cut: Vodafone, BXMT, SBLK, FMC

Concern for dividend cut - FSK, TCPC, PNNT, PTMN now (BCIC), KEN.

Earnings poor or unpredictable: TCPC, SBLK, KEN

Over valuation/ capital gains: ATT, BITX, TEN-E, CSCO

Take Over bid: DBRG

Cash parking for yield and need to use: JBBB

Called or could be called: RITM- A, DLNG-B, SEAL- A, TEN-E.

It can be seen in the chart above that there is a real mix of high and low yield, which makes investing and replacement income quite interesting and important for managing the portfolio yield itself.

The reasons for buying are primarily for quality and replacement income.

MSFT was added for its “AAA” S&P credit rating and advancements into artificial intelligence.

Finance was replaced with finance and some interesting cryptocurrency ETFs, ETHU, SSK and BITO.

The real estate sector also increased and admittedly, there is one addition to Alexandria that I must watch closely as it already had a dividend cut to start 2026.

The remainder not mentioned as additions might just have to be another article and will not all be addressed in this one.

Summary/Conclusion

RIG finished 2025 up 12.92% with a 5.8% yield and a forward estimated yield of 6.3%. There were other trades that are not listed, as I did some other manipulating of trimming and adding on that don’t show up in total portfolio change. The total investment total is 77 to end 2025.
